case_name: Kum. H. Supriya vs S. Krishnappa
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
facts: The respondent No.1/plaintiff instituted O.S.No.7/2018 for specific performance of an alleged sale agreement dated 14.09.2018 against defendant Nos.1 to 3. The petitioner (defendant No.3) filed I.A.No.VIII during cross-examination of PW1 seeking permission under Order VI Rule 17 CPC to amend the written statement by adding paragraph 17A to incorporate subsequent events, including matters in O.S.No.59/2021. The trial Court rejected the amendment application on 17.11.2025 on the ground that the petitioner had not exercised due diligence in seeking amendment earlier. The petitioner challenged that order under Article 227 by filing this writ petition.
arguments_claimant: The petitioner (defendant No.3) argued that the proposed amendment relates to subsequent events that arose after the written statement was filed and therefore could not have been pleaded earlier; she sought amendment under Order VI Rule 17 read with Section 151 CPC. She contended that allowing the amendment would not prejudice the plaintiff because the plaintiff could file a rejoinder/reply to the amended pleading.
arguments_respondent: The respondent No.1 (plaintiff) opposed the amendment on the ground of delay and lack of due diligence, arguing that the petitioner should have sought amendment earlier and that permitting it at the stage of trial would be prejudicial. The trial Court accepted that position and rejected the amendment application.
ratio_decidendi: The High Court held that amendment should be permitted where the proposed pleading relates to "subsequent events that are alleged to have occurred after institution of the suit" which "obviously could not have been pleaded" at the time of filing the original written statement. Relying on precedents of the Apex Court and a co-ordinate Bench, the Court found that the trial Court's refusal to allow the amendment "has occasioned failure of justice warranting interference by this Court." The Court emphasized that allowing the amendment would not cause prejudice because "the plaintiff would have an opportunity to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement," and therefore the trial Court's conclusion was erroneous. Accordingly, the appropriate exercise of jurisdiction under Article 227 is to set aside the impugned order and permit the amendment to prevent injustice.
final_order: The writ petition is allowed; the impugned order dated 17.11.2025 rejecting I.A.No.VIII in O.S.No.7/2018 is set aside and I.A.No.VIII under Order VI Rule 17 CPC is allowed. Liberty is given to the plaintiff to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement and the trial Court is directed to dispose of the suit within one year; all rival contentions are kept open.
summary: This is a writ petition under Article 227 in which defendant No.3 challenged the trial Court's refusal to allow an application to amend the written statement to include subsequent events. The High Court held that the proposed amendment related to events occurring after the original pleading and that refusal to permit it caused a failure of justice. The Court allowed the amendment, set aside the trial Court's order, gave the plaintiff liberty to file rejoinder, and directed the trial Court to decide the suit within one year.
embedding_summary: Kum. H. Supriya v. S. Krishnappa, High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru, WP No.37035 of 2025 (NC:2026:KHC:7501), judgment dated 9 February 2026. Writ under Article 227 challenging order dated 17.11.2025 in O.S.No.7/2018 rejecting I.A.No.VIII by defendant No.3 to amend written statement under Order VI Rule 17 CPC and Section 151 CPC; amendment sought to incorporate subsequent events including matters in O.S.No.59/2021. Court relied on LIC v. Sanjeev Builders (2022) 16 SCC 1, Dinesh Goyal @ pappu v. Suman Agarwal (2024 INSC 726) and a coordinate Bench decision (Shri Mohammadrafi v. Bandenawaz W.P.108512/2025). Held that refusal to allow amendment "has occasioned failure of justice warranting interference" and that allowing amendment causes "no prejudice" as plaintiff can file rejoinder; petition allowed, impugned order set aside, amendment permitted and trial Court directed to dispose suit within one year.
legal_principles: Amendment allowed to plead "subsequent events" occurring after pleadings under Order VI Rule 17 CPC
Refusal to allow amendment that causes a "failure of justice" is liable to be interfered with under Article 227
Lack of prior diligence is not fatal where the events sought to be pleaded arose after the original pleading
Prejudice to the opposing party is avoidable by permitting rejoinder/reply to the amended pleading
Trial Court must consider substantive justice over strict procedural bar when amendment does not unfairly prejudice the other side
cases_cited: LIC v. Sanjeev Builders (P) Ltd. - (2022) 16 SCC 1
Dinesh Goyal @ pappu v. Suman Agarwal (bindal) & ors. - 2024 INSC 726
Shri Mohammadrafi and Anr. Vs. Bandenawaz and Ors - W.P.108512/2025 dated 16.12.2025
bm25_text: Kum. H. Supriya vs S. Krishnappa High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru 2026 The respondent No.1/plaintiff instituted O.S.No.7/2018 for specific performance of an alleged sale agreement dated 14.09.2018 against defendant Nos.1 to 3. The petitioner (defendant No.3) filed I.A.No.VIII during cross-examination of PW1 seeking permission under Order VI Rule 17 CPC to amend the written statement by adding paragraph 17A to incorporate subsequent events, including matters in O.S.No.59/2021. The trial Court rejected the amendment application on 17.11.2025 on the ground that the petitioner had not exercised due diligence in seeking amendment earlier. The petitioner challenged that order under Article 227 by filing this writ petition. The petitioner (defendant No.3) argued that the proposed amendment relates to subsequent events that arose after the written statement was filed and therefore could not have been pleaded earlier; she sought amendment under Order VI Rule 17 read with Section 151 CPC. She contended that allowing the amendment would not prejudice the plaintiff because the plaintiff could file a rejoinder/reply to the amended pleading. The respondent No.1 (plaintiff) opposed the amendment on the ground of delay and lack of due diligence, arguing that the petitioner should have sought amendment earlier and that permitting it at the stage of trial would be prejudicial. The trial Court accepted that position and rejected the amendment application. The High Court held that amendment should be permitted where the proposed pleading relates to "subsequent events that are alleged to have occurred after institution of the suit" which "obviously could not have been pleaded" at the time of filing the original written statement. Relying on precedents of the Apex Court and a co-ordinate Bench, the Court found that the trial Court's refusal to allow the amendment "has occasioned failure of justice warranting interference by this Court." The Court emphasized that allowing the amendment would not cause prejudice because "the plaintiff would have an opportunity to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement," and therefore the trial Court's conclusion was erroneous. Accordingly, the appropriate exercise of jurisdiction under Article 227 is to set aside the impugned order and permit the amendment to prevent injustice. The writ petition is allowed; the impugned order dated 17.11.2025 rejecting I.A.No.VIII in O.S.No.7/2018 is set aside and I.A.No.VIII under Order VI Rule 17 CPC is allowed. Liberty is given to the plaintiff to file rejoinder/reply to the amended written statement and the trial Court is directed to dispose of the suit within one year; all rival contentions are kept open. The High Court held that the proposed amendment related to events occurring after the original pleading and that refusal to permit it caused a failure of justice. The Court allowed the amendment, set aside the trial Court's order, gave the plaintiff liberty to file rejoinder, and directed the trial Court to decide the suit within one year. Kum. H. Supriya v.
